text: The surgeon begins the procedure by developing a preperitoneal flap, carefully separating the tissue layers to access the surgical site.
The surgeon identifies an indirect hernia on the left side with no visible mesh. The silhouette of a mesh is observed on the contralateral side, not extending beyond the midline.
The surgeon uses heat to perform a bloodless dissection in the preperitoneal space, carefully assessing for adhesions and avoiding blunt dissection to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection, unaffected by previous operations, carefully developing the area before proceeding to the medial dissection, which may show signs of compromise from prior surgical interventions.
The surgeon continues the dissection without difficulty, revealing the medial aspect of the contralateral mesh, which is positioned well away from the current dissection site.
The surgeon ensures visibility of the contralateral pubis to establish a landing zone for mesh placement. With medial and lateral dissections completed, the focus shifts to reducing the hernia sac.
The surgeon focuses on reducing the hernia sac, carefully dissecting away surrounding tissue to ensure a clear separation and facilitate an easy dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects distally to bring down surrounding tissue, identifying the beginning of the hernia sac and spermatic structures. A well-demarcated edge of the sac is visible, which is reduced with ease.
The surgeon focuses on developing a dissection to establish a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, ensuring precise identification of anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon carefully dissects the surrounding structures to parietalize the vas and vasculature, with the vas clearly visible in the surgical field.

The previously dissected space is visible with the mesh positioned on top of the bladder, creating a clear landing zone. Extra tissue is observed falling into the femoral canal, although the patient does not have a femoral hernia.
The surgeon takes down aerial tissue fibers, exposing the external iliac artery as it enters the femoral canal, while developing the surrounding space.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection, revealing the iliopubic tract. There is no visible cord lipoma in the area.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to further develop the lateral space, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully grasps the spermatic cord structures, gently teasing the peritoneum downward. The pre-peritoneal aspect of the medial umbilical ligament is dissected to allow the vas deferens to retract over the external iliac vein.
The surgeon has established a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, ensuring that the anatomical structures within this area are well-defined and visible.
The surgeon points out anatomical landmarks, indicating an imaginary line from Cooper's ligament to the lateral sidewall. A mid-weight, extra-large mesh is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the mesh over Cooper's ligament and secures it with a single stitch.
The corner of the mid-weight, extra-large mesh is visible, slightly protruding from its intended position.

The surgeon adjusts the corner of the mesh back into its proper position, ensuring it is securely placed to prevent clam shelling and recurrence. A standard flap closure is performed, and another hole below the mesh is identified for subsequent closure.
